note:
很少有其他职业能够带着立即创造自己工作所需的技能离开工作岗位。我们别浪费时间了。
在我大学的第一周，我在娱乐中心的游泳池里。大学的第一周是神奇的，因为每个人都处于同样的位置：从头开始，重新开始。每个人都在向每个人做自我介绍，
我和我宿舍的朋友在游泳池里。新鲜的友谊，但我们觉得我们已经认识很多年了。我们正从跳板上跳下来。没什么花哨的，这里没有奥运选手。有人做了一个后空翻，我下定决心，在那天晚上游泳池关闭之前，我要做我有生以来的第一个后空翻。
当你教自己做后空翻时，你必须忽略大脑中的一部分，“如果我们这样做，我们会落在脖子上受伤。”你必须看着后墙，双臂向后抛，然后向前，以获得动力，用力推离甲板，真正专注于把你的脚摆动到你的前面，然后再越过。如果你惊慌失措并向前看，你会做一些奇怪的180度半转体，然后倒着落入水中。
但是一旦你打破了大脑中的障碍，内化了机制，意识到你不会因为落入池水而不是坚硬、不可原谅的表面而摔断脖子，你就可以自由地在你的余生中后空翻。
我在大学最后一个学期开始编写代码，用iPad自学Rails书，一次一页。我停下来修改变量、值和事物的名称，以了解哪些是重要的，哪些不重要。
我被制造东西的欲望所驱使。把我脑子里的东西100%带到手机或电脑屏幕上。当我第一次感觉到手中的一款应用程序之前只是我在笔记本纸上画的一个想法时，我在公寓里跳来跳去，兴奋地大叫起来。
一旦我搬到旧金山，我就被所有你可以工作的地方的品牌名称迷住了，风投文化，资金数字，增长率，头衔。
如果你不想太多，你可以从一家公司跳槽到另一家公司，在你的整个职业生涯中都是这个创业公司的名字，而不会四处张望。
我刚满28岁，就和妻子坐下来深入思考我真正想要的是什么。我是一家刚毕业的YC公司的工程总监，我们即将结束我们的首轮比赛，我不开心，工作过度，我知道是时候做一些不同的事情了。
我又回到了拿着我手掌上做的那个东西在博尔德的公寓里跳来跳去的感觉。完全是我的，不是别人眼中的超常成功者。
当你为足够多的创始人工作后，你会意识到他们中的大多数人并没有那么特别。他们很聪明，很敬业，但你也一样。他们研究系统，大量阅读，以便更好地掌握他们的手艺，但你也是如此。
他们和你唯一的区别是，他们比你走得更远。
借用我的朋友内森的话说，作为工程师，我们可以说的是，很少有其他职业能真正说出我们掌握着创造的手段。
在任何时候，我们都可以离开一家科技公司，建立自己的科技公司。或者是一种产品。或者一种功能，有一天可能会成为一种产品，有一天可能会成为一家公司。
一旦你觉得自己具备了成为创始人的必备技能，或者至少有能力在需要的时候学习这些技能，你就不需要在另一家公司工作了。您可以自己构建和启动某些东西-任何东西！
这就是我19岁时的感受，我只是还没有合适的语言。
这就是我将要在这里写的关于更好的编程和未来的东西：为自己写代码，制造我自己的产品，以及我们作为工程师拥有的巨大力量，可以自己制造创收产品，或者与我们选择的一个小团队一起制造创收产品，而不需要资金，也没有任何人的许可。
在我职业生涯的这个阶段，我几乎可以为任何人编写代码。我这么说不是为了吹嘘，我这么说只是为了展示我的选择。
note_en:
Few other professions can walk away from their work with the skills needed to create their own job immediately. Let’s not waste it
   In my first week of college I was at the swimming pool at the Rec Center. The first week of college is magical because everyone is in the same position: clean slate, fresh start. Everyone is introducing themselves to everyone,
  I was at the pool with friends from my dorm. Fresh friendships but we felt like we’d known each other for years. We were jumping off the diving board. Nothing fancy, no Olympians here. Someone did a backflip and I became determined that before the pool closed down that night, I was going to do my first ever backflip.
 When you teach yourself to do a backflip, you have to override a part of your brain that says, “If we do this, we’re going to land on our neck and get hurt.” You have to look at the back wall, throw your arms back and then forward to gain momentum, push hard off the deck, and really focus on swinging your feet it front of you and then over. If you panic and look forward, you’re going to do some weird half-twist-180 and land upside down in the water.
 But once you break that barrier in your brain, internalize the mechanics, and realize that you’re not going to break your neck because you’re landing in a pool of water and not a hard, unforgiving surface, you’re free to backflip for the rest of your life.
  I started writing code in my last semester of college, teaching myself out of a Rails book on my iPad, one page at a time. I paused to hack and change variables values and the names of things to understand what was important and what wasn’t.
 I was driven by a desire to make things. To bring 100% of what was in my head onto the phone or computer screen. The first time I felt an app in my hand that had previously just been an idea I’d sketched on some notebook paper, I jumped around my apartment, yelling in excitement.
  Once I moved to San Francisco, I got caught up in the brand names of all of the places you could work, the VC culture, the funding numbers, the growth rates, the titles.
 If you don’t think about it too hard, you can bounce from company to company, being Your Name From This Startup for your whole career and never look around.
  Right after I turned 28, I sat down with my wife for a deep think about what I really wanted. I was the Director of Engineering at a recent-graduate YC company, we were about to close our Series A, and I was unhappy, over-worked, and I knew it was time for something different.
 I returned to the feeling of jumping around my apartment in Boulder with that thing that I made in the palm of my hand. Fully mine, not being an over-achiever in someone else’s vision.
  When you’ve worked for enough founders, you realize that most of them aren’t that special. They’re smart and dedicated, but so are you. They study systems and and read a lot in order to get better at their craft, but so do you.
 The only difference between them and you is that they went further than you’ve gone so far.
  The thing that we can say as engineers that few other professions can truly say is that we hold the means of creation, to borrow a phrase from my friend  Nathan.
 At any point, we can walk away from a tech company to build our own tech company. Or a product. Or a feature that may one day be a product that may one day be a company.
 And once you feel like you have enough of the requisite skills to be a founder, or at least the ability to learn them as you need them, you don’t need to work at another company. You can build and launch something — anything — yourself!
 That was what I felt at 19. I just didn’t have the language for it yet.
  So that’s what I’m going to be writing about here on Better Programming and into the future: writing code for yourself, building my own products, and the immense power that we have as engineers to build revenue-generating products on our own or with a small team of our choosing, without funding, and without anyone’s permission.
 At this point in my career I can write code for just about anyone. I don’t say that to brag, I say that just to lay out my options.
title: 我们放弃了作为工程师为他人工作的权力
title_en: We Throw Away Our Power as Engineers Working for Other People

note:
“你就是你的驾照、信用卡、医疗卡、楼房通行卡。”
冠状病毒大流行正在蔓延，航空公司和乘客都措手不及。世界上大多数国家，包括美国，开始拒绝外国游客，不想把更多的病毒带过边境。一些飞机在半空中掉头飞回原来的位置。
到了4月份，航空公司和机场面临着一个严峻的现实：没有人乘坐飞机，收入直线下降。空荡荡的机场场景在新闻中变得司空见惯。OneZero通过公共记录请求获得的一条内部信息显示，在美国第二大机场洛杉矶国际机场(Los Angeles International Airport)，离境旅客数量与2019年同期相比下降了95%。航空股暴跌，一些城市开始救助机场特许摊位和汽车租赁公司。
对于为航空公司乘客提供服务的身份验证公司Clear来说，经济放缓似乎是一个事关生死存亡的威胁。Clear的500万会员支付年度订阅费，让他们在美国60多个机场和体育场更快地通过安检线。会员每年支付179美元进入自助服务亭，通过虹膜或指纹扫描仪验证自己的身份，以跳过安检站的长队，任何人都可以注册该应用程序的免费级别，这给予他们在体育场的优惠待遇。OneZero查阅的文件显示，Clear来自公司运营的一些机场的收入在4月份减少了一半以上。Clear至少需要三个月的时间才能恢复接近大流行前的收入。
但OneZero通过公共记录请求获得的3500多份文件和电子邮件揭示了该公司如何利用大流行来转移战略，并帮助将其商业模式扩展到让传单走在前列之外。Clear对其指纹、虹膜扫描和面部识别业务的愿景超出了机场或体育区的售货亭-它希望成为一个全面的身份验证平台，覆盖我们日常生活中更亲密的时刻。该公司已经积累了大量客户的个人数据，特别是那些使用这项服务购买特许权和进入体育场馆的明确客户。该公司甚至出于营销目的，探索与合作伙伴共享这些数据。作为畅通无阻地插到队伍前面的回报，客户交出了大量的生物识别和旅行数据。
在其核心，Clear将信任货币化。当该公司核实一个人的身份时，无论是进入机场、体育场，还是在特许摊位购买啤酒，Clear都是在确认他们就是他们所说的那个人。目前，这一验证过程意味着优先作为受信任的Clear成员进入机场或体育场安检线。但在未来，OneZero查看的文件和幻灯片表明，公司有明确的计划，每当你刷信用卡、在门口出示身份证或交出医疗保险卡时，都会验证你的身份。
该公司首席执行官卡林·塞德曼-贝克尔(Caryn Seidman-Becker)也说了同样的话。(虽然Clear的公共事务主管玛丽亚·科梅拉(Maria Comella)通过电子邮件回答了问题，但该公司没有让塞德曼-贝克尔(Seidman-Becker)参与本文。)。
“我们是一家平台公司…。可以把它想象成亚马逊。一旦你注册了，你就一直在点击一键，“塞德曼-贝克尔在去年美国商会活动的一次台上采访中说。“在机场注册一次：现在你可以在赫兹使用它，现在你可以在体育场使用它，现在你可以在海鹰队用它来买啤酒。这就是平台的力量。现在你考虑增加酒店，现在你考虑拼车…。当你注册时，你就是你的信用卡。“
[Clear]希望成为一个全方位的身份认证平台，覆盖我们日常生活中更多的亲密时刻。
为此，Clear在5月份宣布了一款名为Health Pass的新产品。它采用Clear的主要身份验证服务，并将一个人的健康信息附加到他们的个人资料中。Clear公司的新闻稿称，该公司已向以前从未与其有过正式关系的企业推销该产品，包括美国国家曲棍球联盟(National Hockey League)、创始人桌(Founders Table)等餐饮公司，以及9/11纪念和博物馆(9/11 Memorial and Museum)。
Health Pass的推销对象也是熟悉的客户。根据OneZero查看的文件，7月7日，Clear向洛杉矶国际机场(Los Angeles International Airport)的高管发送了一封电子邮件，通过16张幻灯片的演示确立了其在大流行期间的全球安全愿景。
这张牌被称为“帮助推动美国前进”，它展示了Clear的新产品将如何发挥作用。通过自我管理的健康测验和与Covid测试实验室的集成，Clear的应用程序将允许公司监控写字楼、酒店、餐馆、医院、体育赛事，当然还有机场的员工和顾客的健康状况。只有那些能够证明健康状况良好的人才能进入这些公共生活领域，Clear的应用程序是仲裁者。
Clear诞生于灾难之中。Clear成立于9/11袭击之后，最初被称为身份验证通行证，它利用了国会为保护美国机场免受恐怖主义阴谋而拨出的一波新资金。
该公司并没有忘记这段历史。在最近关于Health Pass的报告中，Clear将冠状病毒的影响与9/11恐怖袭击进行了比较。
“Clear[原文如此]是在9/11事件之后成立的，目的是帮助美国人感到安全可靠地登上飞机。我们值得信赖的选择加入服务处于有利地位，可以迎接筛选2.0的挑战。“。
在2000年代中期，运输安全管理局需要帮助推出“注册旅行者”计划，并将一些旅行者的预筛选工作外包给私人公司。这就是最初运行Clear计划的身份验证通行证开始运行的地方。(今天，Clear是唯一一家在运输安全管理局的注册旅行者计划中获得批准的公司。)
验证身份通行证(VIP)是史蒂文·布里尔(Steven Brill)的创意，他曾是一名法律和安全记者和企业家，他也恰好创立了法院电视(Court TV)。
该公司网站在2007年表示：“在后9-11时代，我们必须采取新的措施来保护自己，同时又不能通过扼杀人员和商业的自由流动来破坏我们的生活方式。”“不知何故，我们必须找到常识性的解决方案，来解决让每个人都成为嫌疑犯的安全瓶颈问题。坦率地说，这意味着我们需要一种公平、明智的方式，在保护恐怖主义方面不会对每个人一视同仁。“。
VIP看起来和今天的明朗有点不同。会员不是用虹膜或指纹扫描来验证你的身份，而是携带一张特殊的卡，他们会在机场的售货亭插入。根据Business Insider的数据，布里尔筹集了超过1亿美元，到本世纪头十年末，估计有20万人加入了VIP计划。在2008年的巅峰时期，VIP在20多个机场运营。
但那一年，该公司举步维艰。一台包含3.3万名会员个人信息的未加密公司笔记本电脑被盗，TSA暂停了该公司在机场注册新客户的能力约两周。(塞德曼-贝克尔现在表示，这一事件是“都市神话”，笔记本电脑上没有任何个人信息。)。
布里尔于2009年3月离开公司，到年底，Verify Identity Pass已经申请破产。
几个月后，两位前10亿美元对冲基金经理在经济衰退后关闭了自己的基金，以600万美元的价格收购了VIP，并提出了一个新想法：将这项服务恢复为一个奢侈科技品牌。Clear计划将不再只是节省你的时间-现在，它将开启一种生活方式。卡林·塞德曼-贝克尔(Caryn Seidman-Becker)和肯·科尼克(Ken Cornick)现在分别是该公司的首席执行官和总裁，他们将公司的名称改为Clear，并将其注册在一家名为Alclear的母公司名下。
Clear还聘请了一些顾问，以提高其在航空业的可信度。在2016年对明尼阿波利斯-圣保罗机场的介绍中，该公司表示，其安全顾问委员会成员包括前特勤局局长马克·沙利文(Mark Sullivan)、前联邦调查局(FBI)局长蒂姆·墨菲(Tim Murphy)、前TSA Gale Rosside副局长，以及乔治·W·布什(George W.Bush)负责国家安全和国土安全顾问的第一助理司法部长肯尼斯·温斯坦(Kenneth Wainstein)。
自重生以来，Clear一直被誉为一个典型的商业成功故事：2018年，塞德曼-贝克尔被评为Fast Company最具创造力的人之一，并被誉为康泰纳仕旅行者创新者(CondéNast Traveler Innovator)。她也是福布斯撰稿人网络上一篇题为“超女确实存在，她看起来很像这位CEO”的文章的主题。
今天，Clear在美国35个机场运营，其售货亭扫描顾客的虹膜和指纹，并将他们送到安检队伍的前线。这项服务被标榜为在长途旅行中压力最大的时候节省时间的奢侈品。
OneZero收集的文件揭示了该公司独特商业模式背后的细节。Clear向机场提供旅客签约订阅时赚取的收入的一部分。根据OneZero获得的Clear与五个主要机场之间的收入分成协议，这些交易通常占Clear在机场营收的10%至12%不等，包括在特定地理区域内的机场门外进行Clear签约。而且这不仅仅是一次性的削减：只要客户保持他们的明确订阅，并从他们的家乡机场起飞，机场每年都会获得一部分费用。
这些收入分享协议有可能每年为机场带来数百万美元的收入。根据OneZero查阅的文件，2019年，该公司将3500万美元的收入返还给了合作机场。
例如：根据OneZero获得的2019年9月的一封电子邮件，Clear预计该公司当年将从洛杉矶国际机场的订阅中赚取2500万美元，其中310万美元将退还给洛杉矶国际机场。规模较小的明尼阿波利斯-圣保罗机场在2019年赚取了800多万美元，其中约100万美元支付给了MSP。
至少从2015年开始，该公司就一直与体育场合作，当时它为自己的应用程序推出了一个免费的级别，让用户可以更快地进入旧金山巨人队的棒球场。
根据OneZero查阅的文件，该公司之前曾考虑将从免费和付费用户那里获得的这些客户数据货币化。在2015年向洛杉矶国际机场的一次演示中，Clear展示了它收集的所有进入体育场的客户的数据，标题为“身份仪表板-有价值的营销数据”。这些数据包括体育场馆最喜欢的食物和饮料，他们什么时候到达比赛，他们有什么类型的信用卡，他们和谁一起观看比赛，以及他们乘坐头等舱的频率。
类似的信息可以在Clear与航空公司、机场、体育场和特许摊位的合作伙伴关系中获得。这里有一个例子：在2018年与西雅图水手T-Mobile体育场特许摊位的合作中，Clear用户可以验证他们超过21岁，并用他们的指纹支付啤酒，只要卡和指纹之前已经在Clear注册过。
该公司之前曾考虑将从免费和付费用户那里获得的这些客户数据货币化。
Clear告诉OneZero，未经用户许可，公司永远不会出售或租赁任何用户信息，也不会向合作伙伴发送信息。该公司的服务条款与这条线相呼应，但确实表示，公司可以使用您的个人数据向您推销他们认为您可能会喜欢的产品。这类似于Facebook不出售数据，而是通过广告向用户提供访问权限，并根据自己的内部系统定制哪些用户可以看到这些广告。
Clear公共事务主管玛丽亚·科梅拉(Maria Comella)告诉OneZero：“如果与合作伙伴分享任何个人信息，都必须征得用户的同意。”
2015年赢得洛杉矶国际机场业务的演示表明，Clear向机场以外扩张的努力甚至在其一些最大的机场合同签署之前就开始了。在演示中，Clear开始将自己定位为一个平台，而不仅仅是一种跳过安检线的方式。
在推销中，Clear强调了其客户的“生态系统”，这意味着Clear客户可以在当地扩展到能够使用这项服务的地方。
幻灯片指向了它所称的旧金山湾区“生态系统”，这是从2012年开始的一系列地区性合作伙伴关系，当时Clear在旧金山国际机场安装了售货亭。2013年，Clear在圣何塞机场增加了售货亭，2014年，SFO的纳帕农场市场特许权摊位增加了触摸支付售货亭。2015年，Clear与旧金山巨人队(San Francisco Giants)和阿拉斯加航空公司(Alaska Airlines)合作，在棒球比赛中增加了Clear更快的入场速度，并在Clear中用指纹取代了一些飞行员的登机牌。
从那时起，Clear在奥克兰田径队的主场环中央体育馆和圣何塞的地震体育场为会员增加了通道。
去年，在美国商会(U.S.Chamber of Commerce)的一次活动中，塞德曼-贝克尔(Seidman-Becker)宣布，该公司甚至与百威(Budweiser)合作制造了一款“Bud Now”机器。她说：“取下指纹，检查你是不是你，你已经21岁了，你有一笔认证付款，它会在不到20秒的时间里把你的啤酒送到你那里。”
塞德曼-贝克尔在2019年5月接受CNBC采访时表示：“我们相信，任何一个你掏出钱包、拿出卡片来证明你是你的地方，都是一个Clear有巨大机会的地方。”“想想电子烟、啤酒和在线游戏的年龄验证吧。”
专注于跟踪生物识别行业的Acuity Market Intelligence的首席分析师C·马克辛·莫斯特(C.Maxine Most)表示，Clear有很大的机会成为其会员的数字身份证，一个存放所有会员信用卡、政府文件、医疗保健信息等的地方。在便利店付款需要扫描你的脸或指纹，而Clear将验证你的身份并提供你的信用卡信息。因为现在，不依赖智能手机的身份平台真的没有竞争。
“我们相信，任何一个你掏出钱包，拿出卡片证明你就是你的地方，都是Clear有很大机会的地方。”
古德智能公司(Goode Intelligence)创始人、生物识别分析师艾伦·古德(Alan Goode)表示，这一想法超越了Apple Pay或点击支付信用卡。这就是生物识别专家所说的“无令牌”或“裸体”验证，因为你唯一需要证明身份的就是你的脸或指尖。
当谈到特许权付款时，这看起来可能是轻率的，但这个想法适用于你钱包里可能随身携带的任何一种身份证明或卡。
塞德曼-贝克尔之前就谈过这个想法，去年甚至预测钱包将会终结，他说，五到七年后，钱包将不再起作用。
“你是你的驾照，你的信用卡，你的医疗卡，你的大楼通行卡--你为什么要掏出所有这些卡？”她说。
Clear在2015年的演示表明，该平台可以与楼宇接入、员工筛选、零工经济甚至P2P贷款集成在一起。所有这些用例都依赖于需要验证身份的想法。
现在到了2020年，Clear正在验证一个比信用卡或登机牌更及时的身份：一个人的健康。
该公司的最新产品Health Pass是Clear开发的一款单独的应用程序，允许企业跟踪员工是否报告了冠状病毒症状或接受了新冠肺炎检测呈阳性。
塞德曼-贝克尔(Seidman-Becker)在6月份接受CNBC采访时表示：“就像9/11事件后，筛查发生了永久性的变化一样，在后Covid时代的环境中，你会看到筛查和公共安全发生了重大变化。”“但这一次是在机场以外的地方。它是体育场，它是零售业，它是写字楼，它是餐馆。“。
根据OneZero获得的文件，根据发给洛杉矶国际机场的演示文稿，Clear正在推销一个世界，在这个世界里，Health Pass可以安装在酒店、医院或任何希望跟踪客户、员工或居民健康的企业或地点。
“如果健康通行证将他们带入企业领域，那将是巨大的，”分析师莫斯特说。她补充说，生物识别公司多年来一直在尝试让指纹和面部识别用于这些用途，但都以失败告终。
西雅图水手队的T-Mobile体育场为Clear的未来报价提供了一个愿景。早在2018年，水手们就允许Clear设立安全通道，成员可以在那里优先进入公园。与此同时，棒球场的一些供应商使用CLEAR来验证客户年龄。该系统整合了Clear的生物识别和支付数据，让会员可以用指纹支付啤酒等优惠，而啤酒不再需要收银员查看身份证。
截至今年夏天，体育场还整合了Health Pass。据水手队棒球场运营副总裁特雷弗·古比(Trevor Gooby)说，每天早上，通勤到体育场的工作人员都必须登录Clear的健康通行证应用程序，并进行一次简短的健康测试。如果他们没有报告任何症状，也没有接受新冠肺炎检测呈阳性，他们就会得到一个绿色的复选标记，当他们到达工作地点时，他们必须显示安全性。
2020年7月，NHL还采用了Health Pass来跟踪检测和自我报告的健康筛查。该计划被誉为成功地保障了球员和工作人员的安全，并为其他联赛树立了榜样。截至7月，纽约市9/11纪念博物馆也在使用Health Pass以及快餐休闲连锁店Chop‘t和Dos Toros。在Clear寄给OneZero的宣传材料中，该公司承诺每周都会宣布新的合作伙伴关系。
塞德曼-贝克尔(Seidman-Becker)去年5月在美国商会(U.S.Chamber of Commerce)的一次活动中发表讲话，衡量了明确订户占城市总人口的百分比。在旧金山和旧金山湾区，6%的居民是明确会员。在丹佛，这一比例超过了5%。
它提醒人们，Clear并不是简单地争夺机场线的一部分。Clear正在发出信号，它打算成为美国人日常生活的一部分，而冠状病毒大流行创造的“新常态”对该公司来说是一线希望。
“当我看到我们的机遇集时，它是巨大的，”她说。他说：“对很多行业来说，身份认同都是基础和基础。你入住一家旅馆，他们一遍又一遍地问你要驾照和信用卡。你就是你。一旦人们采纳了这一点，它就会变得强大起来。“

‘You are your driver’s license, your credit card, your health care card, your building access card’
   The coronavirus pandemic was spreading, and both airlines and passengers were caught unprepared.  Most of the world, including the United States, began turning away foreign visitors, not wanting to bring more of the virus past their borders. Some airplanes were  turned around in midair and sent back to where they’d come from.
 By April, airlines and airports faced  a grave reality: Nobody was flying, and revenue was plummeting. Scenes of  empty airports became common in the news. At Los Angeles International Airport, the second-largest in the U.S., the number of departing passengers dropped by 95% compared to the same time period in 2019, according to an internal message obtained by  OneZero via public records request. Airline stocks plummeted, and some cities began  bailing out airport concession stands and car rental companies.
 For Clear, an identity verification company that serves airline passengers, the slowdown seemed like an existential threat. Clear’s 5 million members pay for an annual subscription that lets them travel through security lines faster at more than 60 airports and sports arenas around the U.S. Members pay $179 a year to access kiosks where they verify their identity via iris or fingerprint scanners in order to skip long lines at security checkpoints, and anyone can sign up for the app’s free tier, which grants them this preferential treatment at stadiums. Clear’s revenue from some of the airports where the company operates more than halved during the month of April, according to documents reviewed by  OneZero. It would take at least three months for Clear to resume generating revenue close to what it was before the pandemic.
 But more than 3,500 documents and emails obtained by  OneZero through public records requests shed light on how the company used the pandemic to pivot and help expand its business model beyond getting fliers to the front of the line. Clear’s vision for its fingerprint, iris-scanning, and facial recognition business goes beyond kiosks in airports or sports areas — it wants to be a holistic identity verification platform, covering more intimate moments in our everyday lives. The company has already amassed troves of personal data on its customers, especially for Clear customers who use the service to buy concessions and enter sports stadiums. The company has even explored sharing that data with partners for marketing purposes. In return for cutting to the front of the line unimpeded, customers handing over vast swaths of biometric and travel data.
 At its core, Clear monetizes trust. When the company verifies a person’s identity, whether that be to enter an airport, a stadium, or buy a beer at a concession stand, Clear is affirming that they are who they say they are. Right now, this verification process means priority access to an airport or stadium security line as a trusted Clear member. But in the future, documents and slideshows reviewed by  OneZero suggest Clear plans to be the company that verifies your identity every time you would have swiped a credit card, shown your ID at a door, or handed over a health insurance card.
 Caryn Seidman-Becker, the company’s CEO, has said as much. (While Clear’s head of public affairs Maria Comella answered questions over email, the company did not make Seidman-Becker available for this story.)
 “We are a platform company… think about it like Amazon. Once you register, you’re tapping One-Click all the time,” Seidman-Becker said during an on-stage interview at a  U.S. Chamber of Commerce event last year. “Enroll once at the airport: now you can use it at Hertz, now you can use it at the sports stadium, now you can use it at the Seahawks to buy a beer. That is the power of a platform. Now you think about adding hotels, now you think about ride-share… You are your credit card when you enroll.”
 [Clear] wants to be a holistic identity verification platform, covering more intimate moments in our everyday lives.
 To that end, Clear  announced a new product called Health Pass in May. It takes Clear’s main identity verification service and attaches a person’s health information to their profile. Clear has pitched the product to businesses that it never had formal relationships with before, including the National Hockey League, restaurant firms like Founders Table, and the 9/11 Memorial and Museum, according to the company’s press releases.
 The Health Pass pitch went to familiar clients, as well. On July 7, Clear sent an email to executives at Los Angeles International Airport, according to documents reviewed by  OneZero, establishing its vision for global security during the pandemic across a 16-slide presentation.
 The deck was called “Helping Get America Moving,” and it laid out how Clear’s new product would work. Through self-administered health quizzes and integrations with Covid testing labs, Clear’s app would allow companies to monitor the health of staff and patrons of office buildings, hotels, restaurants, hospitals, sporting events, and of course, airports. Only those who are able to prove good health would gain access to these realms of public life, with Clear’s app as the arbiter.
  Clear was born of disaster. Founded in the wake of the 9/11 attacks, Clear — originally known as Verified Identity Pass — rode a wave of new funding from Congress allocated to secure American airports from terrorist plots.
 That history isn’t lost on the company. In its recent presentation on Health Pass, Clear compared coronavirus’s impact to the terrorist attacks of 9/11.
 “CLEAR [sic] was founded in the wake of 9/11 to help Americans feel safe and secure getting on airplanes. Our trusted opt-in service is well positioned to meet the challenge of screening 2.0,” the presentation said.
 In the mid-2000s, the TSA needed help launching “ Registered Traveler” programs and outsourced some of the prescreening of travelers to private companies. This is where Verified Identity Pass, which originally operated the Clear program, got its start. (Today, Clear is the only company approved in the TSA’s Registered Traveler program.)
 Verified Identity Pass, or VIP, was the brainchild of Steven Brill, a former law and security journalist and entrepreneur, who also happened to found Court TV.
 “In the post 9–11 era, we had to take new measures to protect ourselves yet not destroy our way of life by strangling the free flow of people and commerce,” the company’s  website said in 2007. “Somehow, we had to find common sense solutions to security bottlenecks that make everyone a suspect. To be blunt, that meant we needed a fair, sensible way not to treat everyone the same when it comes to terrorism protection.”
 VIP looked a bit different from what Clear is today. Instead of verifying your identity with an iris or fingerprint scan, members carried a special card that they would insert at kiosks in an airport. Brill raised  more than $100 million, and an estimated 200,000 people joined the VIP program by the late 2000s, according to  Business Insider. At its peak in 2008, VIP operated in more than 20 airports.
 But that year, the company stumbled. An unencrypted company laptop containing the personal information of 33,000 members  was stolen, and the TSA suspended the company’s ability to register new customers in airports for about two weeks. (Seidman-Becker  now says this episode is an “urban myth” and that there wasn’t any personal information on the laptop.)
 Brill left the company in March 2009, and by the end of the year, Verified Identity Pass had filed for bankruptcy.
 Months later, two former billion-dollar hedge fund managers, having closed their fund in the wake of the recession, bought VIP for just $6 million with a novel idea: rehabilitating the service as a luxury tech brand. The Clear program would no longer just save you time — now, it would unlock a lifestyle. Caryn Seidman-Becker and Ken Cornick, now the company’s CEO and president, respectively, changed the name of the company to Clear, and registered it under a parent company called Alclear.
 Clear also brought on a number of advisers to bring credibility to its role in the airline industry. In a 2016 presentation to Minneapolis-Saint Paul airport, the company said its security advisory board included former director of the Secret Service Mark Sullivan, former FBI director Tim Murphy, former deputy administrator of the TSA Gale Rossides, and Kenneth Wainstein, the first assistant attorney general for national security and homeland security advisor to George W. Bush.
 Since its rebirth, Clear has been lauded as a quintessential business success story: In 2018, Seidman-Becker was named one of  Fast Company’s  Most Creative People and hailed as a  Condé Nast Traveler  Innovator. She was also the subject of an article on  Forbes’ contributor network headlined “ Superwoman Does Exist, And She Looks A Lot Like This CEO.”
  Today, Clear operates in 35 airports in the U.S., where its kiosks scan the irises and fingerprints of customers and send them to the front of security lines. The service is billed as a luxury time-saver during one of the most stressful points in a long trip.
 Documents gathered by  OneZero reveal details behind the company’s unique business model. Clear offers airports a cut of the revenue earned when a traveler signs up for a subscription. These deals typically range from 10% to 12% of Clear’s revenue at the airport, and include Clear sign-ups outside of the airport’s doors within a specific geographic zone, according to revenue sharing agreements between Clear and five major airports obtained by  OneZero. And it’s not just a one-time cut: As long as the customers maintain their Clear subscription and fly from their home airport, the airport gets a piece of the fee every year.
 These revenue sharing agreements have the potential to bring in millions every year for an airport. In 2019, according to documents reviewed by  OneZero, the company gave $35 million of its revenue back to partner airports.
 For example: According to an email from September 2019 obtained by  OneZero, Clear projected that it would earn $25 million from subscriptions at Los Angeles International Airport that year, $3.1 million of which would be sent back to LAX. The smaller Minneapolis-Saint Paul airport earned Clear more than $8 million in 2019, about $1 million of which was paid out to MSP.
 The company has also been partnering with stadiums since  at least 2015 when it launched a free tier to its app that would allow users to more quickly enter the San Francisco Giants’ ballpark.
  According to documents reviewed by  OneZero,  the company has previously considered monetizing this customer data that it obtained from its free and paid users. In a 2015 presentation to LAX, Clear showed off all the data that it collected on customers who enter stadiums, with the title “Identity Dashboard — Valuable Marketing Data.” That data includes favorite foods and beverages at sports stadiums, when they arrive at games, what kind of credit card they have, whom they attend games with, and how often they fly first class.
 Similar information could be obtained within the partnerships Clear has with airlines, airports, stadiums, and concession stands. Here’s an example: In a  2018 partnership with concession stands at the Seattle Mariners’ T-Mobile stadium, Clear users could verify they’re over 21 and pay for a beer with their fingerprint, as long as the card and fingerprint had been previously registered with Clear.
 The company has previously considered monetizing this customer data that it obtained from its free and paid users.
 Clear told  OneZero the company would never sell or rent any user information, or send information to partners, without asking users permission. The company’s terms of service echo this line, but does say the company can use your personal data to market products to you that they think you might like. This is similar to the way that  Facebook doesn’t sell data, but instead offers access to its users via ads, and tailors which users see those ads based on its own internal system.
 “If any personal information is shared with a partner, it is only with the user’s consent,” Maria Comella, Clear’s head of public affairs, told  OneZero.
  A 2015 presentation to win LAX’s business illustrates that Clear’s push to expand beyond airports began even before some of its biggest airport contracts were signed. In the presentation, Clear started pitching itself as a platform rather than just a way to skip the security line.
 In the pitch, Clear emphasized an “ecosystem” for its customers, meaning a local expansion of where Clear customers are able to use the service.
 The slides pointed to what it called the Bay Area “ecosystem,” a series of regional partnerships starting in 2012 when Clear installed kiosks in the San Francisco International Airport. In 2013, Clear added kiosks to San Jose Airport, and in 2014, touch-to-pay kiosks at the Napa Farms Market concession stand at SFO. In 2015, Clear partnered with the San Francisco Giants and Alaska Airlines to add Clear’s quicker entry to baseball games and replace  some flyers’ boarding passes with their fingerprints through Clear.
  Since then, Clear has added access lanes for members at the RingCentral Coliseum, home of the Oakland Athletics, and San Jose’s Earthquakes Stadium.
 Last year, at a U.S. Chamber of Commerce event, Seidman-Becker announced that the company had even partnered with Budweiser to make a “Bud Now” machine. “Fingerprints down, checks you are you, you are over 21, and you have an authenticated payment, and it delivers you your beer, all in less than 20 seconds,” she said.
 “We believe any place that you are whipping out your wallet and taking out a card to prove that you are you is a place where Clear has big opportunities,” Seidman-Becker  told  CNBC in May 2019. “Think about the age validation for e-cigarettes, beer, and online gaming.”
  C. Maxine Most, principal analyst at Acuity Market Intelligence, which focuses on tracking the biometrics industry, says that there’s a wide opportunity for Clear to become its members’ digital identification card, the one place that holds all of its members’ credit cards, government documents, health care information, and more. To pay at a convenience store would be scanning your face or fingerprint, and Clear would verify your identity and supply your credit card information. Because right now, there’s really no competition for an identity platform that doesn’t rely on a smartphone.
 “We believe any place that you are whipping out your wallet and taking out a card to prove that you are you is a place where Clear has big opportunities.”
 The idea goes a step beyond Apple Pay or tap-to-pay credit cards, says Alan Goode, biometrics analyst and founder of Goode Intelligence. It’s what biometrics wonks call “tokenless” or “naked” verification because the only thing you need to prove your identity is your face or your fingertips.
 That might seem frivolous when talking about concession payments, but the idea scales to any kind of identification or card that you might carry around in your wallet.
 Seidman-Becker has spoken around this very idea before, and last year even predicted the end of wallets, saying in five to seven years they wouldn’t serve a purpose anymore.
 “You are your driver's license, your credit card, your health care card, your building access card — why are you whipping all these cards out?” she said.
 Clear’s 2015 presentation illustrates that the platform could be integrated with building access, employee screening, the gig economy, and even peer-to-peer lending. All of these use cases rely on the idea that an identity needs to be verified.
 Now in 2020, there’s a piece of identity that Clear is verifying that’s more timely than a credit card or boarding pass: a person’s health.
 The company’s latest product, Health Pass, is a separate app built by Clear that allows a business to track whether employees have reported coronavirus symptoms or received a positive Covid-19 test.
  “Just like screening was forever changed post-9/11, in a post-Covid environment you’re going to see screening and public safety significantly shift,” Seidman-Becker  said on CNBC in June. “But this time it’s beyond airports. It’s sports stadiums, it’s retail, it’s office buildings, it’s restaurants.”
 According to documents obtained by  OneZero, Clear is pitching a world in which Health Pass could be installed in hotels, hospitals, or any business or location that wants to keep track of the health of its customers, employees, or residents, according to the presentation sent to LAX.
 “If the Health Pass is getting them into the enterprise space, that’s huge,” analyst Most says. She adds that biometrics companies have been trying and failing to get fingerprint and facial recognition to work for these kinds of uses for years.
  Seattle Mariners’ T-Mobile Stadium offers a vision for what Clear’s future offers could look like. Back in  2018, the Mariners allowed Clear to set up security lanes, where members were able to get priority access to the park. At the same time, some of the ballpark’s vendors used Clear to verify customer age. The system integrates Clear’s biometrics and payment data, and lets members pay for concessions like beer, which no longer requires a cashier to look at an ID, with their fingerprints.
 As of this summer, the stadium has also integrated Health Pass. Every morning, staff who commute to the stadium have to log into Clear’s Health Pass app and take a short health quiz, according to Trevor Gooby, Mariners VP of ballpark operations. If they don’t report any symptoms, and haven’t received a positive Covid-19 test, they get a green check mark, which they will have to show security when they arrive at work.
 In July 2020, the  NHL also adopted Health Pass to track testing and self-reported health screenings. The program was lauded as  successful at keeping players and staff safe and a model for other leagues. As of July, New York City’s 9/11 Memorial Museum is also using Health Pass as well as fast-casual restaurant chains Chop’t and Dos Toros. And in promotional materials sent to  OneZero by Clear, the company promises that new partnerships are being announced every week.
 Speaking at a U.S. Chamber of Commerce event last May, Seidman-Becker measured Clear subscribers as percentages of the total city population. In San Francisco and the Bay Area, 6% of total residents were Clear members. In Denver, it was more than 5%.
 It served as a reminder that Clear isn’t simply competing for a share of the airport line. Clear is signaling that it intends to be part of the fabric of Americans’ daily life, and the coronavirus pandemic’s creation of a “new normal” has, for the company, a silver lining.
 “When I look at our opportunity set, it is enormous,” she said. “Identity is fundamental and foundational to so many industries. You check into a hotel and they ask you for your driver's license and credit card over and over again. You are you. And once people adopt that, it’s powerful.”
title: 文件显示，允许其成员跳过美国35个机场的安检线的文件显示，该公司正在建立一个数字身份证系统，用于存储医疗保健信息等
title_en: Documents show how Clear, which lets its members skip security lines at 35 US airports, is building a digital ID system for storing health care info and more

note:
编者按，2012年4月24日更新：我们收到了很多关于这篇文章的评论和问题，你可以在这里阅读。罗伯特后来对这篇报道进行了更新，并对其进行了更多细节和修改。
因此，有一位宇航员在太空中环游地球，他确信自己再也回不了地球了；他正在与当时的苏联高级官员阿列克谢·科西金(Alexei Kosygin)通电话，后者正在哭泣，因为他也认为这位宇航员会死。
太空船的构造粗制滥造，燃料耗尽到危险的程度；它的降落伞--尽管没有人知道--没有成功，宇航员弗拉基米尔·科马罗夫(Vladimir Komarov)即将全速撞向地球，他的身体在撞击时会熔化。当他走向末日的时候，美国在土耳其的监听站听到他愤怒地哭泣，咒骂那些把他送进一艘拙劣的宇宙飞船里的人。
关于1967年一名俄罗斯宇航员死亡的这一异常亲密的描述出现在杰米·多兰(Jamie Doran)和皮尔斯·比佐尼(Piers Bizony)的新书“星人”(Starman)中，该书将于下个月出版。作者的叙述主要基于克格勃官员维亚明·伊万诺维奇·鲁萨耶夫(Venyamin Ivanovich Russayev)的爆料，以及雅罗斯拉夫·戈洛瓦诺夫(Yarola V Golovanov)之前在普拉夫达(Pravda)的报道。这个版本--如果它是真的--令人震惊。
斯塔曼讲述了两名宇航员弗拉基米尔·卡马罗夫(Vladimir Kamarov)和苏联英雄尤里·加加林(Yuri Gagarin)之间的友谊故事，尤里·加加林是第一个到达外太空的人类。这两个人很亲密，他们一起社交，一起打猎，一起喝酒。
1967年，两人被分配执行同样的绕地飞行任务，两人都知道太空舱飞行不安全。科马罗夫告诉朋友，他知道自己可能会死。但他不会退缩，因为他不想让加加林死。加加林本来会是他的接班人。
故事开始于1967年左右，当时苏联领导人列昂尼德·勃列日涅夫(Leonid Brezhnev)决定在两艘苏联宇宙飞船之间上演一场壮观的中空会合。
该计划是发射一个装有科马罗夫的联盟一号太空舱。第二天，第二辆车将起飞，另外还有两名宇航员；两辆车将会合，停靠，科马罗夫将从一辆车爬到另一辆车，与一名同事交换位置，然后乘坐第二艘船回家。勃列日涅夫希望，这将是苏联在共产主义革命50周年之际的胜利。勃列日涅夫非常明确地表示，他希望这种情况发生。
问题出在加加林。他已经是苏联英雄，也是有史以来第一个进入太空的人，他和一些高级技术人员检查了联盟1号，发现了203个结构问题-这些严重问题将使这台机器在太空中航行变得危险。加加林建议，任务应该推迟。
问题是：谁会告诉勃列日涅夫？加加林写了一份10页的备忘录，并把它交给了他在克格勃最好的朋友文雅明·鲁萨耶夫(Venyamin Russayev)，但没有人敢把它送上指挥链。所有看到这份备忘录的人，包括鲁萨耶夫，都被降职、解雇或送往外交西伯利亚。距离发射还有不到一个月的时间，科马罗夫意识到推迟发射不是一种选择。他会见了现已降职的克格勃特工鲁萨耶夫(Russayev)，并说：“我不打算从这趟航班上回来了。”
鲁萨耶夫问道，为什么不拒绝呢？根据两位作者的说法，科马罗夫回答说：如果我赶不上这次飞行，他们会派后备飞行员来代替。那是尤里·加加林(Yuri Gagarin)。弗拉基米尔·科马罗夫不能这样对待他的朋友。这就是尤拉，书中引用他的话说，他将代替我死去。我们得照顾他。科马罗夫随后失声痛哭。
1967年4月23日，发射当天，俄罗斯记者雅罗斯拉夫·戈洛瓦诺夫(Yarola V Golovanov)报道称，加加林出现在发射场，要求穿上宇航服，尽管没有人料到他会飞起来。戈洛瓦诺夫称这种行为是突如其来的反复无常，尽管后来一些观察家认为加加林试图强行登上飞机来救他的朋友。联盟号载着科马罗夫离开了地球。
一旦联盟号开始绕地球飞行，失败就开始了。天线没有正确打开。电力遭到破坏。航行被证明是困难的。第二天&的发布不得不取消。更糟糕的是，科马罗夫安全返回地球的机会正在迅速减少。
在此期间，美国情报机构一直在监听。国家安全局在伊斯坦布尔附近的一个空军基地有一个设施。之前的报道说，美国听众知道有些不对劲，但听不清台词。在这篇报道中，一位在书中被确认为佩里·费尔沃克(Perry Fellwock)的美国国家安全局分析师描述说，他无意中听到科马罗夫告诉地面控制官员，他知道自己即将死亡。费尔沃克描述了苏联总理阿列克谢·科西金(Alexei Kosygin)是如何打电话告诉他自己是英雄的。科马罗夫的妻子也在电话中谈论该对他们的孩子说些什么。科西金在哭。
当太空舱开始下降，降落伞未能打开时，这本书描述了美国情报机构如何在科马罗夫跳下死亡时听到他愤怒的哭声。
在互联网上(亚马逊(Amazon.com)89美分)，我找到了可能是科马罗夫的遗言：
一些译者听到他说，太空舱里的热量正在上升。他还用了“杀死”这个词--大概是为了描述工程师们对他做了什么。
在20世纪60年代的太空竞赛中，双方都知道这些任务是危险的。我们有时会忘记有多危险。同年1月，1967年，美国人格斯·格里森、埃德·怀特和罗杰·查菲死于阿波罗太空舱内的火灾。
两年后，当美国人登上月球时，尼克松政府发表了一份由演讲稿撰稿人威廉·萨菲尔(William Safire)准备的以防万一的声明，宣布尼尔·阿姆斯特朗(Neil Armstrong)和巴兹·奥尔德林(Buzz Aldrin)的死亡，如果他们被放逐或杀害的话。死亡并不是意想不到的。
但弗拉基米尔·科马罗夫的死似乎几乎是照本宣科。尤里·加加林(Yuri Gagarin)在坠机几周后接受“真理报”(Pravda)采访时说了同样的话。他严厉批评了放飞他朋友的官员。
科马罗夫参加了国葬。只有一块脚后跟的碎骨在坠机中幸存下来。三周后，尤里·加加林去见他的克格勃朋友。他想谈谈发生的事。正如书中所描述的那样：
然后，作者提到了一个从未被证实的谣言(在我看来，这是最不可能的)，有一天加加林确实和勃列日涅夫有过一段时间，他把一杯饮料扔到了勃列日涅夫的脸上。
尤里·加加林(Yuri Gagarin)在1968年死于一场空难，也就是美国人登月的前一年。
杰米·多兰(Jamie Doran)和皮尔斯·比佐尼(Piers Bizony)的书是“斯塔曼：尤里·加加林传奇背后的真相”(Starman：The Truth Beend of Yuri Gagarin，Walker出版社，2011年)；雅罗斯拉夫·戈洛瓦诺夫(Yarola V Golovanov)对尤里·加加林的采访发表在1989年6月11日的“真理报”(KomSomolskaya，Pravda)。维亚明·鲁萨耶夫关于加加林和科马罗夫的故事于2006年出现在“文学报”上，并在几个网站上转载。
note_en:
Editor's Note, Updated April 24, 2012: We received many comments and questions on this post, which  you can read here. Robert later wrote  this update to this story with more details and amendments.
  So there's a cosmonaut up in space, circling the globe, convinced he will never make it back to Earth; he's on the phone with Alexei Kosygin — then a high official of the Soviet Union — who is crying because he, too, thinks the cosmonaut will die.
    The space vehicle is shoddily constructed, running dangerously low on fuel; its parachutes — though no one knows this — won't work and the cosmonaut, Vladimir Komarov, is about to, literally, crash full speed into Earth, his body turning molten on impact. As he heads to his doom, U.S. listening posts in Turkey hear him crying in rage, "cursing the people who had put him inside a botched spaceship."
  This extraordinarily intimate account of the 1967 death of a Russian cosmonaut appears in a new book,  Starman, by Jamie Doran and Piers Bizony, to be published next month. The authors base their narrative principally on revelations from a KGB officer, Venyamin Ivanovich Russayev, and previous reporting by Yaroslav Golovanov in  Pravda. This version — if it's true — is beyond shocking.
      Starman tells the story of a friendship between two cosmonauts, Vladimir Kamarov and Soviet hero Yuri Gagarin, the first human to reach outer space. The two men were close; they socialized, hunted and drank together.
  In 1967, both men were assigned to the same Earth-orbiting mission, and both knew the space capsule was not safe to fly. Komarov told friends he knew he would probably die. But he wouldn't back out because he didn't want Gagarin to die. Gagarin would have been his replacement.
  The story begins around 1967, when Leonid Brezhnev, leader of the Soviet Union, decided to stage a spectacular midspace rendezvous between two Soviet spaceships.
  The plan was to launch a capsule, the Soyuz 1, with Komarov inside. The next day, a second vehicle would take off, with two additional cosmonauts; the two vehicles would meet, dock, Komarov would crawl from one vehicle to the other, exchanging places with a colleague, and come home in the second ship. It would be, Brezhnev hoped, a Soviet triumph on the 50th anniversary of the Communist revolution. Brezhnev made it very clear he wanted this to happen.
  The problem was Gagarin. Already a Soviet hero, the first man ever in space, he and some senior technicians had inspected the Soyuz 1 and had found 203 structural problems — serious problems that would make this machine dangerous to navigate in space. The mission, Gagarin suggested, should be postponed.
     The question was: Who would tell Brezhnev? Gagarin wrote a 10-page memo and gave it to his best friend in the KGB, Venyamin Russayev, but nobody dared send it up the chain of command. Everyone who saw that memo, including Russayev, was demoted, fired or sent to diplomatic Siberia. With less than a month to go before the launch, Komarov realized postponement was not an option. He met with Russayev, the now-demoted KGB agent, and said, "I'm not going to make it back from this flight."
  Russayev asked, Why not refuse? According to the authors, Komarov answered: "If I don't make this flight, they'll send the backup pilot instead." That was Yuri Gagarin. Vladimir Komarov couldn't do that to his friend. "That's Yura," the book quotes him saying, "and he'll die instead of me. We've got to take care of him." Komarov then burst into tears.
  On launch day, April 23, 1967, a Russian journalist, Yaroslav Golovanov, reported that Gagarin showed up at the launch site and demanded to be put into a spacesuit, though no one was expecting him to fly. Golovanov called this behavior "a sudden caprice," though afterward some observers thought Gagarin was trying to muscle onto the flight to save his friend. The Soyuz left Earth with Komarov on board.
  Once the Soyuz began to orbit the Earth, the failures began. Antennas didn't open properly. Power was compromised. Navigation proved difficult. The next day's launch had to be canceled. And worse, Komarov's chances for a safe return to Earth were dwindling fast.
  All the while, U.S. intelligence was listening in. The National Security Agency had a facility at an Air Force base near Istanbul. Previous reports said that U.S. listeners knew something was wrong but couldn't make out the words. In this account, an NSA analyst, identified in the book as Perry Fellwock, described overhearing Komarov tell ground control officials he knew he was about to die. Fellwock described how Soviet premier Alexei Kosygin called on a video phone to tell him he was a hero. Komarov's wife was also on the call to talk about what to say to their children. Kosygin was crying.
  When the capsule began its descent and the parachutes failed to open, the book describes how American intelligence "picked up [Komarov's] cries of rage as he plunged to his death."
    On the Internet ( 89 cents at Amazon.com) I found what may have been Komarov's last words:
  Some translators hear him say, "Heat is rising in the capsule." He also uses the word "killed" — presumably to describe what the engineers had done to him.
    Both sides in the 1960s race to space knew these missions were dangerous. We sometimes forget how dangerous. In January of that same year, 1967, Americans Gus Grissom, Ed White and Roger Chaffee died in a fire inside an Apollo capsule.
    Two years later, when Americans landed on the moon, the Nixon White House had a just-in-case  statement, prepared by speechwriter William Safire, announcing the death of Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin, had they been marooned or killed. Death was not unexpected.
    But Vladimir Komarov's death seems to have been almost scripted. Yuri Gagarin said as much in an interview he gave to  Pravda weeks after the crash. He sharply criticized the officials who had let his friend fly.
  Komarov was honored with a state funeral. Only a chipped heel bone survived the crash. Three weeks later, Yuri Gagarin went to see his KGB friend. He wanted to talk about what happened. As the book describes it:
    The authors then mention a rumor, never proven (and to my mind, most unlikely), that one day Gagarin did have a moment with Brezhnev and he threw a drink in Brezhnev's face.
    Yuri Gagarin died in a plane accident in 1968, a year before the Americans reached the moon.
    Jamie Doran and Piers Bizony's book is Starman: The Truth Behind the Legend of Yuri Gagarin  (Walker Publishing 2011); Yaroslav Golovanov's interview with Yuri Gagarin was published in Komsomolskaya, Pravda , June 11, 1989. Venyamin Russayev's stories about Gagarin and Komarov appeared in 2006 in Literaturnaya Gazeta  and were republished on several websites.
title: 一名宇航员“在愤怒中哭泣”坠毁在地球上
title_en: Cosmonaut Crashed into Earth 'Crying in Rage'

note:
根据苹果公司指控其回收合作伙伴违反其订单的一项新诉讼，包括iPhone和iPad在内的至少10万台注定要被销毁的苹果设备实际上正在使用中。
苹果在加拿大提起的诉讼中声称，加拿大回收公司Geep转售这些设备，而不是将其粉碎，违反了合同。苹果寻求3000万加元(约合2300万美元)。根据诉讼，来自Geep，诉讼于1月份提起，但直到最近才曝光。
但这起诉讼对这家iPhone制造商的环保做法提出了新的质疑，因为苹果公司表示，它从来不会销毁仍然适合使用的电子产品。苹果在诉讼中称，发送给Geep的设备已经“修复”.。但苹果没有解释这些设备是如何修复的，也没有在诉讼中提出任何具体指控，称这些设备损坏或使用不安全。苹果发言人乔希·罗森斯托克(Josh Rosenstock)拒绝透露是否对苹果发送给Geep的设备进行了任何维修，以及这些设备出了什么问题。
修理网站iFixit的创始人凯尔·维恩斯对苹果起诉吉普的消息披露感到惊讶，这起诉讼最先是由加拿大新闻网站The Logic发现的。维恩斯在一封电子邮件中写道：“这太令人惊讶了，因为它把苹果公司的这种可怕的可疑活动暴露在了光天化日之下。”“看看他们的环境报告--他们吹嘘自己在苹果内部找到了可以重复使用的手机！然后他们强迫回收商粉碎完全可用的设备。“。
罗森斯托克在一份电子邮件声明中写道，苹果的产品具有“行业领先的寿命”，该公司“每年都会安全地翻新尽可能多的设备，仅去年一年就超过1000万台。”他写道，只有在“严格的测试”表明产品不符合“行业领先的安全和质量标准”后，产品才会被回收。他补充说，苹果产品的任何材料在被回收后都不会进入垃圾填埋场。
在法庭文件中，吉普否认有不当行为，声称所谓的盗窃是由流氓员工实施的。Geep被另一家回收公司收购，目前已不再运营，该公司正在起诉那些被指控的流氓员工，他们已经离开了公司。记者无法联系到这些前员工置评。
苹果和代表Geep的律师拒绝透露这些设备是否有问题，也拒绝对这些指控发表任何额外评论。
多年来，苹果一直在吹捧其减少环境影响的努力，宣传其回收机器人以及在笔记本电脑中重复使用废铝金属。而且，在最近的一份声明中，它表示到2030年将实现碳中性。该公司表示，为了减少浪费，该公司甚至停止了与部分Apple手表一起发货的电力充电器，行业分析师预计，苹果不会在一些新iPhone上发货耳机或充电器。
苹果为旧设备提供了以旧换新计划，并向用户承诺，它将努力为旧产品找到新的所有者。“你可能已经用完了你的设备，但很可能它还有更多可以提供的东西。如果它完好无损，我们会帮它找个新主人。如果没有，我们会把它寄给我们的回收合作伙伴，“该公司在其网站上说。
但2017年的一项主板调查发现，苹果将大部分回收工作外包给外部公司，并在合同上迫使这些公司“粉碎”旧设备，甚至禁止回收商回收零部件重复使用。
罗森斯托克拒绝透露苹果向外部回收商发送了多少设备，也拒绝透露苹果内部回收了多少设备。他指出，苹果创造了自己的回收机器人，名为黛西和戴夫，其中一款能够每小时拆卸200部iPhone。罗森斯托克说，与外部回收公司相比，苹果拆解苹果产品的方法从设备中回收的材料更多。他不愿透露机器人回收了多少部iPhone。
罗森斯托克也不愿透露苹果用什么标准来确定一台苹果设备是被发送给新用户，还是最终被粉碎或拆卸。
但制造新的消费电子产品对环境有害。根据绿色和平组织的一份报告，从2007年到2016年，智能手机生产消耗了968太瓦时的能源。这大约相当于印度13.5亿人口一整年消耗的能源。手机等设备还需要开采稀土矿物，这与侵犯人权有关，当这种材料污染地下水和其他资源时，可能会对环境产生连锁反应。
回收旧电子设备可能比把它们扔进垃圾填埋场更有利于环境，但电子回收专家表示，很少有部件在新设备中重复使用。罗森斯托克不愿透露回收的iPhone有多少可以重复使用。
环保倡导组织Stand.Earth的全球气候运动主管加里·库克(Gary Cook)表示，苹果在针对Geep的诉讼中“暴露了自己”，披露了他所说的“来之不易”的信息。他说：“他们感兴趣的是试图推动新手机的销售，而不是试图让现有手机继续使用。”
库克和其他人表示，苹果在确保其设备尽可能长时间使用方面做得不好。这首先要从手机的设计方式说起。内存和电池被粘在手机上，使用户无法接触到它们，而且手机没有升级功能，迫使许多消费者比他们通常想要的更早购买新手机。
苹果可以让更换电池、内存、破碎屏幕和其他组件变得容易，就像其他制造商所做的那样。例如，一家名为Fairphone的公司允许其客户升级组件。当发布更好的相机时，客户只需更换旧相机即可。
根据苹果对Geep的诉讼，苹果发现这10万台设备据称仍在使用，因为它检测到它们重新连接到了蜂窝网络。
法庭文件显示，Geep员工据称不遗余力地避免粉碎苹果设备，将它们转移到仓库的一个部分，该仓库避开了摄像头和伪造记录，以掩盖所谓的盗窃行为。诉讼称，许多设备最终被运往中国。
苹果声称，更多的设备可能在苹果不知情的情况下被重复使用，因为它们没有包含手机芯片。
根据法庭文件，Geep对这些设备被转售的指控进行了内部调查。它承认这些设备被重复使用。但吉普的律师约翰·隆戈(John Longo)拒绝透露转售的任何设备是否得到了修复，也拒绝透露它们是否对消费者构成了任何安全问题。
“这些必须撕碎的合约时有发生，让回收商抓狂。他们不想毁掉硬件。“iFixit的维恩斯说。
回收商表示，苹果并不是唯一一家被指控销毁原本可以重复使用的电子产品的公司。由于与消费电子公司有保密协议，拒绝透露姓名的回收商表示，公司有时会销毁可用的产品，因为它们更老了，这会增加销售最新型号的难度。他们说，二手或有瑕疵的产品以较低的价格出售可能会稀释公司的高端品牌。
但回收商说，公司也会销毁那些存在令人尴尬的产品缺陷的设备，他们希望这些缺陷不会被公众发现。有时，产品会有真正的安全问题，比如三星的电池会爆炸。
公司销毁智能手机等物品的另一个原因是，当它们被消费者丢弃时，它们往往包含个人数据。但许多回收商已经想出了删除手机所有数据的方法，以便转售。
电子产品回收公司Hobi International的联合创始人兼总裁克雷格·博斯韦尔(Craig Boswell)说，“我们觉得我们开发的软件工具和销毁一样好。”公司多久会接受他的提议，擦拭、清洁设备并转售它们？“不像我希望的那样频繁，”他说。“我希望百分之百的时间都是这样。”
note_en:
At least 100,000 Apple devices, including iPhones and iPads, that were destined to be destroyed are actually in use, according to a new lawsuit by Apple that accuses its recycling partner of violating its orders.
  Apple claims in its lawsuit, filed in Canada, that Canadian recycling company GEEP violated its contract by reselling the devices, instead of shredding them. Apple is seeking $30 million Canadian (about $23 million U.S.) from GEEP, according the lawsuit, which was filed in January but only recently came to light.
 But the lawsuit raises new questions about the iPhone maker’s environmental practices, because Apple says it never destroys electronics that are still suitable for use. Apple said in the lawsuit that the devices sent to GEEP had been “repaired.” But Apple didn’t explain how they were repaired or make any specific allegations in the suit that the devices were damaged or unsafe to use. Apple spokesman Josh Rosenstock declined to say whether any repairs were made to or what was wrong with the devices Apple sent to GEEP.
   Kyle Wiens, founder of repair website iFixit, was surprised by the revelation of Apple’s lawsuit against GEEP, which was  first uncovered by Canadian news site the Logic. “This is amazing, because it brings this terrible shady activity by Apple out into the daylight,” Wiens wrote in an email. “Look at their environment report — they brag about finding phones internally at Apple that they can reuse! And then they’re forcing recyclers to shred perfectly usable devices.”
 Rosenstock wrote in an emailed statement that Apple products have “industry-leading longevity” and that the company refurbishes “as many devices as we safely can each year, including over 10 million in the last year alone.” Products are recycled only after “rigorous testing” shows they don’t meet “industry-leading safety and quality standards,” he wrote, adding that no material from Apple products ever makes it to a landfill after being recycled.
  In court documents, GEEP denied wrongdoing, claiming the alleged theft was carried out by rogue employees. GEEP, which was acquired by another recycling firm and is no longer in operation, is suing the alleged rogue employees, who have left the company. The former employees could not be reached for comment.
 Apple and lawyers who represent GEEP declined to say whether anything was wrong with the devices or provide any additional comment on the allegations.
  Apple for years has touted its efforts to reduce its environmental impact, advertising its  recycling robots and its reuse of aluminum scrap metal in laptops. And, in a recent announcement, it said it would be  carbon neutral by 2030. It even stopped shipping power chargers with some of its Apple Watches to cut down on waste, the company said, and industry analysts expect that Apple won’t ship headphones or chargers with some new iPhones.
   Apple offers a trade-in program for old devices and promises users it will try to find a new owner for the old products. “You may be done with your device, but chances are it still has more to give. If it’s in good shape, we’ll help it go to a new owner. If not, we’ll send it to our recycling partner,” the company says on its website.
 But a  2017 Motherboard investigation found that Apple outsourced the majority of its recycling to outside companies and that it contractually forced those companies to “shred” the old devices, prohibiting the recyclers even from salvaging components for reuse.
 Rosenstock declined to say how many devices Apple sends to outside recyclers or how many it recycles in-house. He pointed out that Apple created its own recycling robots, called  Daisy and Dave, one of which is able to disassemble 200 iPhones per hour. Rosenstock said Apple’s method for taking apart Apple products recovers more material from the devices than outside recycling companies. He wouldn’t say how many iPhones the robots have recycled.
   Rosenstock also wouldn’t say what criteria Apple uses to determine whether an Apple device gets sent to a new user or ends up shredded or disassembled.
 But making new consumer electronics products is harmful to the environment. Smartphone production consumed 968 terawatt hours of energy from 2007 to 2016, according to a  Greenpeace report. That’s about as much energy as India’s 1.35 billion people consume in an entire year. Devices like phones also require the mining of rare-earth minerals, which have been tied to  human rights abuses and can have ripple effects on the environment when the material pollutes groundwater and other resources.
 Recycling old electronics devices may be better for the environment than throwing them into a landfill, but experts in electronics recycling say very few parts get reused in new devices. Rosenstock won’t say how much of a recycled iPhone gets reused.
   Gary Cook, global climate campaigns director for  Stand.Earth, an environmental advocacy organization, said Apple “outed themselves” in the lawsuit against GEEP, revealing information that he called “hard to come by.” “They’re interested in trying to drive sales of new phones rather than trying to enable the continued use of existing phones,” he said.
  Cook and others say Apple does not do a good job of ensuring its devices are used as long as possible. That starts with how the phones are designed. Memory and batteries are glued into phones, making them inaccessible to owners, and the phones offer no upgrade capabilities, forcing many consumers to buy new phones earlier than they would normally want.
 Apple could make it easy to replace batteries, memory, broken screens and other components, just as other manufacturers do. A company called  Fairphone, for instance, allows its customers to upgrade components. When a better camera is released, customers can simply swap out the old one.
   Apple uncovered that the 100,000 devices were allegedly still in use because it detected that they were reconnected to cellular networks, according to its lawsuit against GEEP.
 GEEP employees allegedly went to great lengths to avoid shredding Apple devices, moving them into a part of the warehouse that avoided cameras and falsifying records to cover up the alleged theft, according to court filings. Many of the devices were eventually sent to China, the lawsuit says.
 Apple alleges that many more devices may have been reused without Apple’s knowledge because they did not contain cellular chips.
 According to court documents, GEEP conducted an internal investigation into allegations that the devices were resold. It admitted that the devices were reused. But John Longo, GEEP’s attorney, declined to say whether any of the devices resold were repaired or whether they posed any safety issue to consumers.
  “These must-shred contracts happen all the time, and it drives recyclers crazy. They don’t want to destroy the hardware,” Wiens of iFixit said.
   Recyclers say Apple isn’t alone in allegedly destroying electronics that could otherwise be reused. Recyclers who declined to be named because they have confidentiality agreements with consumer electronics companies, said companies sometimes destroy usable products because they are older and it would make it more difficult to sell the latest models. Used or blemished products being sold for less money can dilute a company’s premium brand, they say.
 But companies also destroy devices that have embarrassing product defects they hope will not be discovered by the public, recyclers say. And sometimes, products have real safety issues, such as Samsung’s  exploding batteries.
 Another reason companies destroy items like smartphones is that they often contain personal data when they are discarded by consumers. But many recyclers have figured out ways to erase the phones of all their data so that they can be resold.
 “We feel our software tools we develop do an equally good job as destruction,” said Craig Boswell, co-founder and president of Hobi International, an electronics recycling company. How often do companies take him up on the offer to wipe clean the devices and resell them? “Not as often as I’d like,” he said. “I’d like it 100 percent of the time.”

我知道这不是讲课的Subreddit，但也可能是最需要它的。
作为一个物种，我们需要尽我们所能，确保我们不会在我们人类造成的地球加速变暖问题上走得太远。由于各大智能手机公司每年都发布一款新手机而形成的文化是不道德的，也是不负责任的。在不久的将来，每年我们都需要升级我们的设备的想法正在对我们、我们的星球和我们的后代造成巨大的伤害。
想想看，制造一部新手机所需的资源之多令人难以置信。从设计阶段到生产，再到运输和储存。然后是大量的碳排放到大气中。实际上，不要去想这件事。那种规模对我们来说是深不可测的。这完全是疯了。然后我们有这样的评论：
我得了11分，我得了12分，我觉得很不错。
&#34；我有一台11Pro，我正在升级到12Pro，主要是为了平边。&#34；
在我看来，这种心态是犯罪的，根本没有考虑到不久的将来，甚至现在的后果。
要知道，我们大多数人生活在一个基本自由的世界里，我们有很多选择，有很多奢侈品，有很多东西可以买，只要我们有足够的资源，我们随时都可以做到这一点。但我们需要遏制我们极端和不可持续的购买习惯。
我本来也打算这么做[从11号机上买一台12 Pro]，直到我意识到我已经用新相机拍了最多100张照片，只用A13就能在Instagram和Twitter上滚动出壮观的性能了。
关注最新的泄密和趋势真的很有趣。对于我们中的一些人来说，剥掉新手机的塑料膜的感觉是无与伦比的。我懂了。但下次当你觉得你想要一部仅次于最好的手机时，想想这个问题：你想买的手机是否满足了你目前的手机无法满足的即时需求？需要，例如，一种必需品。一些对你来说是必要的东西。
想想看，这种需求是必须通过购买旗舰产品来满足，还是老一辈的几代人就可以正常工作？或者买一部二手手机？你需要从你的7到12的专业版吗，或者SE2可以很好的工作吗？
我知道这有点脱节，但是谢谢你的阅读。我希望我们可以在评论中讨论一下。
note_en:
I know this is the wrong subreddit to lecture to, but also potentially the one that needs it the most.
 As a species, we need to do what we can to make sure we don't take things too far with regards to the accelerated warming of our planet caused by us humans. The culture that has come about because of every major smartphone company releasing a new phone every year is unethical, and irresponsible. The idea that every year, we need to "upgrade" our devices is doing a huge disservice to us, our planet, and our descendants in the  very near future.
 Think about the incredible amount of resources required to manufacture a new phone. From the design phase to the production, to the shipping and storing. And then the sheer amount of carbon emissions into the atmosphere. Actually, don't think about it. That sort of scale is unfathomable to us. It's completely insane. And then we have comments like this:
 " I have an 11 and I’m getting the 12 pro cause I feel like it"
  "  I have an 11 Pro and I’m upgrading to the 12 basically for the flat edges alone."
  In my opinion, this kind of mentality is criminal, and not at all thinking about the consequences of the near future, and even  right now.
 Understood, most of us live in a mostly free world where we have many options to choose from, many luxuries, and plenty of things to buy, and we can do that whenever we want, provided we have the resources for it. But we need to curb our extreme and unsustainable purchasing habits.
  "I was planning to do exactly same [buy a 12 Pro from an 11] until i realized i’ve taken max like 100 pics with the new camera and just use the A13 for an spectacular performance  scrolling Instagram and Twitter lol"
 Following the latest leaks and trends is really fun. The feeling of peeling the plastic off of a new phone is unmatched for some of us. I get it. But the next time you feel like you want the next best phone, think about this question: Does the phone that you want to buy satisfy an immediate  need that your current phone doesn't?  Need, as in, a necessity. Something that is necessary to you.
 Think about whether that need must be satisfied by purchasing the flagship, or would a couple of generations older work just fine? Or maybe by buying a used phone? Do you need the 12 pro from your 7, or will an SE 2 work just fine?
   I know this is a bit disjointed, but thanks for reading. I hope we can discuss in the comments a bit.

2009年，旧金山的市政预算总额为65亿-86亿美元(按今天的美元计算)，经通胀和人口调整后为86亿美元。旧金山2019年的预算是令人瞠目结舌的122亿美元，仅自2018年以来就增长了10%。然而，这座城市未能将这一惊人的预算增长与资本投资或服务的类似增长相匹配，这为金钱所能做的有限提供了一个实物教训。
像谷歌(Google)、Salesforce和优步(Uber)这样总部位于该市及其周边地区的公司，向当地经济注入了极高的工资和股票期权意外之财，当地经济见证了房地产价值-以及其他所有东西的成本-飙升。市政府的金库里充斥着税收。尽管这种影响在过去十年中最为明显，但它可以追溯到20年前的第一次互联网繁荣-1999年，该市预算为42亿美元，相当于今天的77亿美元。在这20年中，超出通胀和人口增长的超额预算总额达到了惊人的230亿美元。
旧金山用这些财富做了什么？没什么。运营公交车和地铁的市交通局(“MUI”)今年在失败后一直在与失败作斗争。这座城市的住房短缺如此严重，以至于人们不得不住在汽车里，甚至是船里。在过去的6年里，无家可归者增加了14%。肮脏的街道，针头，更糟糕的是人行道上，是去年市长竞选中的一个问题。一座看似富裕到可以用黄金铺路的城市，结果发现它们被垃圾覆盖着。
旧金山已经挥霍了它的财富。它宣称自己是“交通优先”的城市，其密度和地理位置使其成为美国最适合公共交通的城市之一。这座城市本可以用这笔230亿美元的超额资金来建造数十英里的地铁。相反，它只挖了1.6英里的中央地铁，仍然没有开放。旧金山确实在市中心修建了一个火车站--Salesforce运输中心，号称“西部大中央”--只是它没有出资修建一条通往车站的隧道，所以目前还没有火车开往那里，只有公共汽车。
旧金山的政界人士也声称关心经济适用房。即使按照在旧金山湾区建造这样的住房的通胀速度-每套高达70万美元-230亿美元也可以在过去20年里建造3.3万套住房。2018年，该市的补贴住房总数仅约为3.3万套，其中只有3741套来自公共住房或市长办公室等城市项目。其余的来自联邦、州或私人投资。
旧金山还声称非常关心绿色能源和海平面上升。有了230亿美元，它可以免费在城市的每座建筑上安装太阳能电池板，还有近160亿美元的闲置资金用于海防。取而代之的是，旧金山为典型的太阳能安装提供了微不足道的1500美元的信用额度，而海堤只得到了一份“行动计划”和一份“脆弱性和后果评估”。
并不是一直都是这样的。从1917年到1937年的20年间，旧金山挖掘了双峰隧道和日落隧道，这两条隧道仍然是旧金山地铁系统的中心；建成了赫奇·赫奇水库(Hetch Hetchy Library)，它提供了一些美国最干净的水；并修建了金门大桥(Golden Gate)和海湾大桥(Bay Bridges)，这两条大桥将旧金山与该地区连接起来，并维持了它的就业机会。所有这些项目仍在日常使用。
已经有一些成功的故事。这座城市的OneSF首都计划早就应该对街道和下水道进行改善。公用事业委员会已经完成了对Hetch Hetchy供水系统的几次重大地震升级，帮助为最终的大地震做好准备。2008年和2012年的两个公园债券导致了城市公园和游乐场的壮观翻新。但这些在很大程度上代表着良好的治理和渐进的改善，而不是数十亿美元支出的成果。由于没有宏伟的项目可供参考，我们有理由怀疑所有的钱都花到哪里去了。
市政就业占据了很大一部分。在一个家庭收入中位数为96,265美元的城市，工资和福利占预算的近45%，平均每位员工175,004美元。该市雇佣了31830人，每28名居民1人，每个街区6名员工。用于拨款的8.73亿美元-支付给非营利性组织或其他团体用于各种社会服务-几乎相当于基本建设项目和设施维护支出的总和。
然而，更大的问题是政治冷漠和官僚管理不善。旧金山背负着36.3亿美元的本金债务，自2015年以来，这笔债务本可以用数百万美元的闲置来偿还。旧金山的领导人已经习惯了不费吹灰之力就能增长的收入，并发行了超过10亿美元的额外债券，而不是对该市的金融未来进行长期投资。目前的预算现在背负着16亿美元的偿债负担。
预算异常增长带来的微不足道的结果也是一个管理不善的故事。中央地铁虽然是世界上最昂贵的地铁项目之一，但资金几乎耗尽；最近开通的地铁又推迟了18个月。去年，穆尼对已有百年历史的双峰隧道进行了关键升级，在施工期间需要额外的公交车来取代火车。市政没有计划额外的司机，而是从其他路线带走了他们，导致该市服务短缺，并导致整个系统的“崩溃”。“西部大中央”虽然没有隧道和轨道，但仍耗资22亿美元，由于结构性裂缝，开放仅6周后就关闭了。这座城市花了200万美元建造了一个公共浴室，每平方英尺4700美元，这一建设成本与高层豪华共管公寓相似。尽管OneSF的资本计划非常成功，但在其网站上搜索项目列表时，会得到这样的信息：“找不到请求的页面。”旧金山表明，投入更多资金将加剧管理不善，而不是解决问题。
旧金山过去20年的预算表明，该市领导层并不真正重视它所说的许多问题-交通、经济适用房、清洁能源。考虑到这座城市年复一年地用其令人难以置信的意外之财几乎没有做什么，它的价值根本不清楚。
菲利普·斯普林辛是美国海军陆战队的老兵，住在旧金山湾区。

In 2009, San Francisco’s municipal budget totaled $6.5 billion—$8.6 billion in today’s dollars, adjusted for inflation and population. San Francisco’s budget for 2019 is  an eye-popping $12.2 billion, a 10 percent increase just since 2018. The city has failed to match this staggering budget growth with a similar increase in capital investment or services, however, providing an object lesson in the limits of what money can do.
 Companies like Google, Salesforce, and Uber, headquartered in and around the city, pour  sky-high salaries and  stock-option windfalls into the local economy, which has seen real estate values—and the cost of everything else—soar. City coffers overflow with tax revenue. Though the effect has been most pronounced for the past decade, it extends as far back as the first dot-com boom, 20 years ago—in 1999, the city budget was $4.2 billion, equivalent to $7.7 billion today. The excess budget above inflation and population growth over those 20 years totals an astonishing $23 billion.
 What has San Francisco done with this wealth? Not much. The Municipal Transportation Agency (“Muni”), which runs the busses and metro, has struggled with  failure  after  failure  this  year. The housing shortage in the city is so bad that it is driving people to live in  cars and even  boats. Homelessness is up 14 percent in the  past 6 years. Dirty streets, with needles and worse on the sidewalks,  were an issue in last year’s mayoral race. A city seemingly rich enough to pave its roads with gold finds them covered in trash.
 San Francisco has squandered its fortune. Proclaiming itself a “Transit First” city, density and geography make it one of the U.S. cities best suited for public transport. The city could have used its $23 billion excess to build dozens of miles of subway. Instead, it dug just 1.6 miles of the Central Subway, still not open. San Francisco did build a downtown train station, the Salesforce Transit Center, billed as the “Grand Central of the West”—except that it didn’t fund a tunnel to the station, so  no trains go there yet, only busses.
 San Francisco politicians also claim to care about affordable housing. Even at the inflated rate to build such housing in the Bay Area—up to  $700,000 per unit—$23 billion could have built 33,000 units in the past 20 years. The total number of subsidized units in the city was only about 33,000 in 2018, and just  3,741 of them came from city programs like public housing or the mayor’s office. The rest came from federal, state, or private investment.
 San Francisco also claims to care greatly about green energy and rising sea levels. With $23 billion, it could have installed solar panels on every building in the city at no cost to the owner, with almost $16 billion to spare for coastal defenses. Instead, San Francisco offers an anemic $1,500 credit for a typical solar installation, and seawalls have gotten no further than an  “action plan” and a “vulnerability and consequences assessment.”
 It wasn’t always this way. In the 20 years from 1917 to 1937, San Francisco dug the Twin Peaks and Sunset tunnels that remain central to its metro system; completed the Hetch Hetchy Reservoir, which provides some of America’s cleanest water; and built both the Golden Gate and Bay Bridges, which connect it to the region and sustain its jobs. All these projects are still used daily.
 There have been some success stories. The city’s OneSF capital plan has been making long overdue improvements to streets and sewers. The Public Utilities Commission has completed several major  seismic upgrades to the Hetch Hetchy water system, helping prepare for the eventual major earthquake.  Two parks bonds, in 2008 and 2012, led to spectacular renovations of city parks and playgrounds. But these largely represent good governance and incremental improvements, not the fruits of billions of dollars of spending. With no grand projects to point to, it’s reasonable to wonder where all the money has gone.
 Municipal employment has eaten up a large share. Salaries and benefits account for almost  45 percent of the budget, averaging $175,004 per employee, in a city where  median household income is $96,265. The city employs 31,830 people, one for every 28 residents and six employees for every city block. The $873 million spent on grants—payments to nonprofits or other groups for various social services—almost equals the total spent on capital projects and facilities maintenance combined.
 The larger issues, however, are political indifference and bureaucratic mismanagement. San Francisco carries principal debt of  $3.63 billion, which could have been paid down since 2015 with millions to spare. Instead of making this long investment in the city’s financial future, San Francisco’s leaders have grown used to revenue that grow without effort and issued more than $1 billion of additional debt. The current budget is now burdened with $1.6 billion of debt service.
 The paltry results from exceptional budget growth are also a story of mismanagement. The Central Subway, though one of the  most expensive subway projects in the world, has almost run out of money; its opening was recently delayed  another 18 months. Last year, Muni made critical upgrades to the century old Twin Peaks tunnel, requiring additional busses to substitute for trains during the work. Muni didn’t plan for the extra drivers and took them from other routes, leaving the city short on service and causing a  system-wide “meltdown.” The “Grand Central of the West,” despite having no tunnel or tracks, still cost $2.2 billion; it  closed only six weeks after opening, due to structural cracks. The city  spent $2 million to build a public bathroom at $4,700 per square foot, a construction cost similar to high-rise luxury condos. As successful as the OneSF capital plan has been, searching for a list of projects on its website returns the message, “ the requested page could not be found.” San Francisco demonstrates that throwing in more money will compound mismanagement, not solve it.
 The story of San Francisco’s budget over the past two decades shows that the city’s leadership doesn’t really value many of the issues—transit, affordable housing, clean energy—that it says it does. Given how little the city has done with its incredible windfall, year after year, it’s not clear what it values at all.
 Phillip Sprincin is a veteran of the United States Marine Corps who lives in the San Francisco Bay Area.

周二，加利福尼亚州的一名联邦法官拒绝了Theranos Inc.的竞标。创始人伊丽莎白·霍姆斯(Elizabeth Holmes)和前高管拉梅什·“桑尼”·巴尔瓦尼(Ramesh“Sunny”Balwani)驳回了他们因与这家声名狼藉的血液测试初创公司有关而面临的刑事指控。
现年36岁的霍姆斯将于3月9日出庭受审，罪名是她和巴尔瓦尼在Theranos血液测试的可靠性上撒谎，骗取投资者数亿美元，并欺骗患者。巴尔瓦尼稍后将单独受审。
两人都对两项电信欺诈共谋和10项电信欺诈指控提出无罪抗辩。如果罪名成立，他们可能面临最高20年的监禁。
霍姆斯的律师没有立即回复记者的置评请求。巴尔瓦尼的律师拒绝置评。
霍姆斯和巴尔瓦尼于2018年6月被加利福尼亚州北区的联邦检察官起诉。在起诉之前，对Theranos进行了长达一年的调查，调查源于“华尔街日报”的文章，揭露了Theranos在技术和商业实践方面的缺陷。
note_en:
A federal judge in California on Tuesday denied bids by Theranos Inc. founder Elizabeth Holmes and former top executive Ramesh “Sunny” Balwani to dismiss the criminal charges they face in connection with the  disgraced blood-testing startup.
  Ms. Holmes, 36 years old, is set to go to trial March 9 on charges she and Mr. Balwani defrauded investors out of hundreds of millions of dollars and deceived patients by lying about the reliability of Theranos blood tests.  Mr. Balwani will face trial separately at a later date.
  Both have pleaded not guilty to two counts of wire-fraud conspiracy and 10 counts of wire fraud. If convicted, they could face up to 20 years in prison.
  Lawyers for Ms. Holmes didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment. A lawyer for Mr. Balwani declined to comment.
  Ms. Holmes and Mr. Balwani were  charged in June 2018 by federal prosecutors in the Northern District of California. The indictments followed a yearslong investigation that grew out of articles in The Wall Street Journal that revealed  failings in Theranos’s technology and business practices.
title: Theranos创始人伊丽莎白·霍姆斯(Elizabeth Holmes)驳回刑事指控的努力失败
title_en: Theranos founder Elizabeth Holmes loses bid to dismiss criminal charges

总部位于华盛顿州雷蒙德市的健身技术公司JAXJOX在新一轮融资中筹集了1000万美元，用于支付其标志性的InteractiveStudio健身设备的研发费用。
首轮融资包括投资者道盖特资本有限公司(Dowgate Capital Ltd.)和企业家奈杰尔·雷(Nigel Wray)，使这家成立3年的公司的总融资达到1,700万美元。
JAXJOX即将发布其InteractiveStudio智能健身房，这是一个家庭健身系统，包括数字可调整的重量，设备中内置的支持人工智能的互联技术，以及直播和点播课程。
有了智能壶铃等独立的自由重量设备内置的联网技术，用户不必站在离屏幕一定距离的地方就可以跟踪形状和动作。
创始人兼首席执行官斯蒂芬·奥武苏(Stephen Owusu)在一份新闻稿中表示：“通过监控绩效指标和使用人工智能，我们可以让用户更全面地了解他们的健康状况，并提供改善他们福祉的建议。”“我们相信，对于使用者来说，举起时产生的跟踪力将变得与跑步时追踪心率一样重要。”
InteractiveStudio可在JAXJOX网站上预购，零售价为2199美元，每月订阅费为39美元。今年秋天，该系统还将作为与百思买(Best Buy)独家零售合作伙伴关系的一部分进行销售。
note_en:
JAXJOX, the Redmond, Wash.-based fitness technology company, has raised $10 million in a new funding round to help pay for the research and development of its signature InteractiveStudio workout equipment.
 The Series A round included investors Dowgate Capital Ltd. and entrepreneur Nigel Wray, and brings total funding to $17 million for the 3-year-old company.
 JAXJOX is getting set to release its InteractiveStudio smart gym, a home fitness system that includes digitally adjustable weights, AI-enabled connected tech built into the equipment, and live and on-demand classes.
  With connected tech built into individual pieces of free-weight equipment, such as a smart kettlebell, users don’t have to stand a certain distance from a screen to have form and motion tracked.
 “By monitoring performance metrics and using AI, we can give users a more holistic view of their health and provide recommendations on improving their wellbeing,” founder and CEO Stephen Owusu said in a  news release. “We believe that, for users, tracking power generated while lifting will become as important as tracking your heart rate while running.”
 The InteractiveStudio is available for pre-order on the JAXJOX website and retails for  $2,199 with a  $39 monthly subscription. The system will also sell as part of an exclusive retail partnership this fall with Best Buy.
title: 健身科技公司JAXJOX筹集1000万美元的首轮融资，准备将其InteractiveStudio锻炼系统运往百思买(BestBuy)门店
title_en: Fitness tech company JAXJOX raises $10M Series A as it gets ready to ship its InteractiveStudio workout system to BestBuy stores
